tema: Modals of Deduction — Past (must have / might have / can't have)

vocabulario.traduccion: no puede haber (casi seguro que no)

banco_oraciones.oracion: Ben must have dropped his phone in the sea again.
Someone might have taken the wrong bag by mistake.
She could have forgotten the meeting.
They must have left already — the lights are off.
It can't have been Sofía; she was with me all afternoon.
He can't have finished already — it's only been five minutes.
They might not have received the message.
Could he have missed the bus?
Do you think she might have forgotten?
Might they have left without us?
Did Ben lose it on purpose? He can't have — he loved that phone.
Could she have called earlier? She might have.
Did they forget the address? They must have.

interaccion: Kenji: Ben's not answering his phone.
Sofía: He **must have lost** it again.
Kenji: It's only been a week since the last one!
Abuela Carmen: He **can't have learned** his lesson yet, then.

banner_diagnostico.opcion_a: He must lost his phone yesterday.
banner_diagnostico.opcion_b: He must have lost his phone yesterday.

inicio.definicion_pragmatica: To guess about something in the past, add 'have' between the modal and the past participle — must have, might have, can't have.

ejercicios.pregunta: It ___ have been Sofía, she was with me.
They ___ have left without us — maybe.

ejercicios.afirmacion: 'Must have + participle' guesses about the past.
'Can't have' is the negative form of 'must have' for deduction.

ejercicios.texto: She ___ have forgotten the meeting. (almost sure)
It ___ have been him, he was working. (almost sure NOT)

ejercicios.respuesta: he must have dropped his phone again

ejercicios.texto_con_error: He must lost his phone yesterday.
ejercicios.respuesta: He must have lost his phone yesterday.

ejercicios.oracion_base: I'm almost sure Ben dropped his phone.
ejercicios.respuesta: Ben must have dropped his phone.

ejercicios.oracion_base: I'm almost sure it wasn't Sofía.

ejercicios.respuesta: They might have missed the bus.

ejercicios.pregunta: Do you think Ben lost his phone on purpose? (short answer, No)

guion: Kenji: He must have lost his phone again — it's the third time this year.
Sofía: Or his battery might have died. He never charges it.
Abuela Carmen: It can't have died, he charged it at my house this morning.
Kenji: Then he must have dropped it somewhere. Classic Ben.

comprension.pregunta: What does Kenji think happened to Ben's phone?
Why can't the battery have died, according to Abuela Carmen?
opciones: He charged it at her house that morning
comprension.respuesta: He charged it at her house that morning

reading.texto: By the time Ben finally called back, everyone had a theory. Sofía was sure he must have left his phone at the beach — again. Kenji thought he might have dropped it on the bus, since Ben always sits by the window with the door open. Abuela Carmen refused to guess: 'He can't have lost it the same way twice,' she said, 'even Ben isn't that unlucky.' When Ben finally explained, the truth was worse than any of their guesses: he had lent it to a stranger to make a call and never got it back. 'You must have known better than that,' Abuela Carmen sighed.

preguntas.afirmacion: Sofía thought Ben must have left his phone at the beach.
Kenji was sure Ben sold his phone.
Ben actually lent his phone to a stranger.
Abuela Carmen believed he lost it the same way as before.
